original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 359279?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 359279): Symptom: After a virtual machine is migrated (vMotion) from one data center to another in a static VXLAN environment, the VM cannot reach its gateway or external destinations, resulting in ping timeouts and connection failures. Scenario: In some scenarios, the switch can fail to program tunnel to VNI associations, leading to traffic loss. Workaround: Remove the affected VNI from the configuration on the switches in the source data center and then add the VNI to the configuration again.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 359666?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 359666): Symptom: With hpe-relay core dump legacy bootp client does not get IP. Scenario: When the DHCP relay daemon processes a legacy BOOTP client packet that lacks the option-53 field, it fails to properly validate the packet's structure before attempting to access this field, leading to a crash. This issue occurs exclusively when the smart-relay feature is enabled. Workaround: Disable the global DHCP smart-relay configuration if not required using no dhcp-smart-relay.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in Multicast Bug 342612?
original_answer: Multicast (Bug ID 342612): Symptom: When switching between IPTV channels, there is an intermittent delay of 2-6 seconds before the new video stream is displayed to the end user. Scenario: The system experiences a small delay in the program of a new multicast flow after receiving the IGMP report. During this time, critical initial video packets containing SPS (Sequence Parameter Set) and PPS (Picture Parameter Set) information may be dropped. These packets are essential for the decoder to properly display the H.264 video stream. Workaround: Configure static IGMP groups for the multicast streams using static igmp join <multicast-group-address> interface <decoder-port>. This ensures the hardware is pre-programmed with the multicast flows, eliminating the join latency.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in REST Bug 353178?
original_answer: REST (Bug ID 353178): Symptom: When uploading firmware using the Web UI or REST API, the system displays the following error message despite the firmware being successfully transferred to the device: There was an error uploading new firmware. Firmware image is invalid. Please enter a valid firmware image. Scenario: When logging console is enabled in the switch configuration and a user attempts to upload firmware through the Web UI or REST API, the upload process is completed, but an HTTP 500 error is generated. Workaround: Until upgrading to the fixed version, use one of the following methods: 1. Disable console logging by removing logging console from the configuration. Then attempt the firmware upload using the Web UI or REST API again. 2. Use TFTP for firmware upload by uploading the firmware using TFTP or SFTP instead of the Web UI or REST API. Example command: copy tftp/sftp://[server-ip]/[firmware-file] primary vrf mgmt.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in VLANs Bug 348612?
original_answer: VLANs (Bug ID 348612): Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in device-mode instead of client-mode as follows: interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for Audio-Video Bridging Bug 312492?
original_answer: Audio-Video Bridging (Bug ID 312492): Symptom: A higher neighbor propagation delay will be observed when port speed is set to 2.5G. As a result, generalized Precision Time Protocol (gPTP) capability will become False and clients will not come up. Scenario: This issue can occur while changing port speeds to 2.5G on a 8360 switch using gPTP. Workaround: Use 1G/5G/10G port speeds instead.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for TFTP Bug 269619?
original_answer: TFTP (Bug ID 269619): Symptom: TFTP Software image upload/download transfer operation fails. Scenario: Downloading/uploading the software Image via sm ubuntu IPv6 TFTP server fails. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for MPLS Bug 194381?
original_answer: MPLS (Bug ID 194381): Symptom: Extended RTs (via ext communities) received from a local side PE are not installed on the remote side PE. Scenario: In an L3VPN network, the extended RTs that are used as route attributes in the VPNv4 routes will not get installed on the other side PE. If those values are used to influence the route selection or policy, it may not work. Workaround: Use standard communities, other BGP attributes locally on the remote side to influence the route selection or policy.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what issue was resolved in ARP Bug 351751?
original_answer: ARP (Bug ID 351751): Symptom: After a server failover, the new MAC address is not updated on the remote VTEP VSX secondary node. Scenario: During a server failover, the remote VTEP VSX primary node correctly updates with the new MAC address. However, the VSX secondary node retains the previous MAC address, leading to an inconsistency between the two nodes. Workaround: Run clear arp.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what issue was resolved in Boot Process Bug 319610?
original_answer: Boot Process (Bug ID 319610): Symptom: When switching between IPTV channels, there is an intermittent delay of 2-6 seconds before the new video stream is displayed to the end user. Scenario: The system experiences a small delay in the program of a new multicast flow after receiving the IGMP report. During this time, critical initial video packets containing SPS (Sequence Parameter Set) and PPS (Picture Parameter Set) information may be dropped. These packets are essential for the decoder to properly display the H.264 video stream. Workaround: Configure static IGMP groups for the multicast streams as follows: static igmp join <multicast-group-address> interface <decoder-port> This ensures the hardware is pre-programmed with the multicast flows, eliminating the join latency. configure interface IP and Active gateway IP/MAC when interface is UP and running.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what issue was resolved in RADIUS Bug 363074?
original_answer: RADIUS (Bug ID 363074): Symptom: The switch intermittently fails to include the Framed-IP attribute in RADIUS accounting packets. This results in missing IP address information for authenticated clients. Scenario: The issue is observed when a client disconnects and reconnects after a short interval (for example, 90 seconds). The Framed-IP attribute may be missing even though the client has a valid IP address and is visible in other system outputs. Workaround: Ensure continuous client traffic after reconnection to allow the switch to relearn the IP address. Alternatively, re-enable MAC authentication on the port or perform a shut/no shut cycle to restore expected behavior.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for Audio-Video Bridging Bug 312492?
original_answer: Audio-Video Bridging (Bug ID 312492): Symptom: A higher neighbor propagation delay will be observed when port speed is set to 2.5G. As a result, generalized Precision Time Protocol (gPTP) capability will become False and clients will not come up. Scenario: This issue can occur while changing port speeds to 2.5G on a 8360 switch using gPTP. Workaround: Use 1G/5G/10G port speeds instead.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for TFTP Bug 269619?
original_answer: TFTP (Bug ID 269619): Symptom: TFTP Software image upload/download transfer operation fails. Scenario: Downloading/uploading the software Image via sm ubuntu IPv6 TFTP server fails. Workaround: Use the blocksize option in the copy command with a blocksize of 1375 or less. For example: Copy copy tftp://[20:1::100];blocksize=1375/image.swi secondary vrf vrf1.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for MPLS Bug 194381?
original_answer: MPLS (Bug ID 194381): Symptom: Extended RTs (via ext communities) received from a local side PE are not installed on the remote side PE. Scenario: In an L3VPN network, the extended RTs that are used as route attributes in the VPNv4 routes will not get installed on the other side PE. If those values are used to influence the route selection or policy, it may not work. Workaround: Use standard communities, other BGP attributes locally on the remote side to influence the route selection or policy.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what issue was resolved in L3 Routes Bug 365274?
original_answer: L3 Routes (Bug ID 365274): Symptom: The Network Management Systems does not display IPv4 addresses for Aruba CX switches running AOS-CX version 10.13. Scenario: This issue occurs when the NMS queries the switch using SNMP and relies on either of the following OIDs to retrieve IPv4 address information: 1.3.6.1.2.1.4.20.1.2 (deprecated) 1.3.6.1.2.1.4.34.1.5 (current but not accessible in 10.13) In AOS-CX 10.13, neither OID returns valid IPv4 data, resulting in incomplete device visibility.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what known issue is documented for Audio-Video Bridging Bug 312492?
original_answer: Audio-Video Bridging (Bug ID 312492): Symptom: A higher neighbor propagation delay will be observed when port speed is set to 2.5G. As a result, generalized Precision Time Protocol (gPTP) capability will become False and clients will not come up. Scenario: This issue can occur while changing port speeds to 2.5G on a 8360 switch using gPTP. Workaround: Use 1G/5G/10G port speeds instead.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what known issue is documented for TFTP Bug 269619?
original_answer: TFTP (Bug ID 269619): Symptom: TFTP Software image upload/download transfer operation fails. Scenario: Downloading/uploading the software Image via sm ubuntu IPv6 TFTP server fails. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, what issue was resolved in MSTP Bug 348612?
original_answer: MSTP (Bug ID 348612): Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in "device-mode" instead of "client-mode":{color:#000000}interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device{color}.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, what issue was resolved in NTP Bug 371717?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 371717): Symptom: Excessive connection lost and connection established logging when multiple NTP servers are configured. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, what issue was resolved in Physical Interfaces Bug 373334?
original_answer: Physical Interfaces (Bug ID 373334): Symptom: Switch-Transceiver is in down state with reason "Module interface error.". Scenario: This issue occurs when flow control is enabled on a particular interface and bi-directional traffic is sent at line rate of 99%. When a shut and no shut (link up down) is done at a particular interface module, interface error is seen. Workaround: Call pv_network_ports_enable(node_id, _port, False) after disabling TM using pv_tm_port_disable.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1050, what issue was resolved in L3 routes Bug 375775?
original_answer: L3 routes (Bug ID 375775): Symptom: Traffic loss in an SVI with vsx active-forwarding enabled, when vsx active-forwarding is disabled/removed on a entirely different SVI. Scenario: In a configuration where vsx active-forwarding is enabled, a special HW resource must be programmed for the VSX peers to handle traffic destined to the active-gateway IP. The trigger for this programming is tied to the router MAC programming), and the ref counting for these router MACs would decrement early, removing the special HW resource and causing traffic loss to occur depending on which peer it lands on. Workaround: There are two possible workarounds: 1. Unconfigure and reconfigure vsx active-forwarding in the SVI. 2. Save the configuration and reboot the VSX peers.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what issue was resolved in Slot Management Bug 384894?
original_answer: Slot Management (Bug ID 384894): Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what issue was resolved in NTP Bug 371717?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 371717): Symptom: Excessive connection lost and connection established logging when multiple NTP servers are configured. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what issue was resolved in L3 Addressing Bug 383988?
original_answer: L3 Addressing (Bug ID 383988): Symptom: Traffic loss in an SVI with vsx active-forwarding enabled when traffic lands on the other peer. Scenario: In a VSX topology with at least 2 SVIs configured with active-forwarding enabled, if vsx active-forwarding is deconfigured in one of the SVIs, then unicast traffic in that VLAN landing on secondary is inadvertently dropped. This leads to other features being affected, such as BFD and OSPFv3. In such a case, the OSPFv3 neighbor adjacency is stuck in EXSTART state. Workaround: There are two known workarounds to fix the traffic loss: 1. Unconfigure and reconfigure vsx active-forwarding in the other SVI. 2. Save the config and reboot the VSX peers.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what known issue is documented for Slot Management Bug 384894?
original_answer: Slot Management (Bug ID 384894): Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in CPU Rx Bug 277160?
original_answer: CPU Rx (Bug ID 277160): Symptom: Traffic to the CPU will be adversely limited potentially below CoPP limits when the rate-limit unknown-unicast configuration element is added at a low rate. Scenario: The switch will not receive a portion of its CPU traffic. As a result, L2 learning, ARP resolution, route learning, and multicast learning will all be impacted. Workaround: Avoid using the rate-limit unknown-multicast configuration element, or raise the rate such that it does not interfere with CPU traffic.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in MSTP Bug 348612?
original_answer: MSTP (Bug ID 348612): Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in "device-mode" instead of "client-mode":{color:#000000}interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device{color}.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in Logging Bug 358847?
original_answer: Logging (Bug ID 358847): Symptom: A VSF stack member switch unexpectedly reboots due to the ops-switchd process crashing with a signal: 6 error. Scenario: In some scenarios with excessive ACL event logging, a crash may occur due to a failure in the system when processing a high volume of ACL log entries. Workaround: Minimize the use of the log keyword in ACL entries, especially on high-traffic rules. Consider using ACL counters instead of logging for monitoring traffic patterns.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in EVPN Bug 364576?
original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 364576): Symptom: Missing ARP entries showing as "incomplete" in the ARP table, causing connectivity issues in remote VTEP. Scenario: This issue may occur in an EVPN VXLAN environment with VSX pairs when a VIP (Virtual IP) moves from one node to another node, MAC address aging occurs or after executing "clear mac-address" on the switch. Workaround: A temporary workaround is to add a static ARP entry for the affected IP addresses.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 371954?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 371954): Symptom: In an L2VPN network, when a DHCP client sends an IP request with the broadcast flag unset (unicast), duplicate DHCP client packets are observed at the DHCP server-one from each VTEP. As a result, the client does not receive an IP address. Scenario: To prevent duplicate DHCP client packets from each VTEP in an L2VPN topology, it is recommended to configure "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" on the switch. However, after saving the switch configuration and rebooting, the relay daemon does not correctly apply all configuration settings at boot time, even though the configuration persists in the database. This causes the duplicate packet issue to reappear. Workaround: To restore correct behavior, manually enable and then disable the L2VPN client configuration using "dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" followed by "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients".

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what known issue is documented for MPLS Bug 194381?
original_answer: MPLS (Bug ID 194381): Symptom: Extended RTs (via ext communities) received from a local side PE are not installed on the remote side PE. Scenario: In an L3VPN network, the extended RTs that are used as route attributes in the VPNv4 routes will not get installed on the other side PE. If those values are used to influence the route selection or policy, it may not work. Workaround: Use standard communities, other BGP attributes locally on the remote side to influence the route selection or policy.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, what issue was resolved in Slot Management Bug 384894?
original_answer: Slot Management (Bug ID 384894): Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.
cleaned_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, what issue was resolved in Slot Management Bug 384894?
cleaned_answer: Slot Management (Bug ID 384894): Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Due to a flaw in how port MAC addresses are assigned, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, what issue was resolved in PVST Bug 389395?
original_answer: PVST (Bug ID 389395): Symptom: An STP port incorrectly transitions into Disabled/Blocked state. Scenario: During certain port flap events, STP may erroneously mark a port as Disabled/Blocked, even when the link should remain active. Workaround: Shut down all affected ports. Restart the hpe-pvstd process using the following commands: start-shell sudo bash ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep systemctl restart hpe-pvstd ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep exit exit

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, what issue was resolved in EVPN Bug 372373?
original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 372373): Symptom: Traffic loss occurs for existing networks in a bundle when the VLAN-aware bundle is disabled or enabled for VLAN-ethernet-tag updates. Scenario: This issue is observed during VXLAN interoperability with Juniper Networks devices, particularly when updating VLAN-ethernet-tag mappings. The problem arises when the entire bundle is disabled, affecting all traffic associated with it. Workaround: Use the same VLAN ID and VNI ID values on both the Juniper and Aruba devices.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what issue was resolved in SNMP Bug 400261?
original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 400261): Symptom: SNMP OID sysUpTimeInstance (1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0) may return an incorrect value when polled, leading to inaccurate uptime reporting in monitoring systems. Scenario: This issue occurs on switches running AOS-CX 10.16 when using SNMP to query sysUpTimeInstance. The reported value does not match the actual system uptime, causing discrepancies in monitoring tools. Workaround: Use the OID hrSystemUptime (1.3.6.1.2.1.25.1.1.0) instead of sysUpTimeInstance for accurate system uptime reporting.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what issue was resolved in L3 Routes Bug 396890?
original_answer: L3 Routes (Bug ID 396890): Symptom: In a VXLAN environment, AOS-CXswitches may fails to install routes in the routing table, causing traffic loss for certain destinations. original_answer: DHCP Server (Bug ID 348727): Symptom: DHCP-Server configuration status becomes stuck in pending state after configuration changes are pushed from Aruba Central, preventing proper DHCP service operation. Scenario: This issue occurs when administrators make configuration changes using Aruba Central, either through Central template groups or the UI group multi-edit option. original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 408309): Symptom: SNMPv3 user password changes do not take effect, after deleting and re-creating the user with a new password, the device still accepts the old password and rejects the new one. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what issue was resolved in SNMP Bug 408309?
original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 408309): Symptom: SNMPv3 user password changes do not take effect, after deleting and re-creating the user with a new password, the device still accepts the old password and rejects the new one. Scenario: This issue may occur when deleting an existing SNMPv3 user and re-adding the same username with a different 'auth-pass'; authentication succeeds only with the original password. Workaround: Create a new SNMPv3 user with the desired password and do not reuse the original username.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what issue was resolved in MAC Tables Bug 415095?
original_answer: MAC Tables (Bug ID 415095): Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur after resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what issue was resolved in OSPFv2 Bug 413286?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 413286): Symptom: OSPF default route in area 0 points to a stub router instead of ASBR via backbone area. Scenario: This issue can occur when there are two paths to the ASBR that generated the external route - one via stub area and another via non-stub area. The external route should get installed with the path reachable through the non-stub area, but the path via stub area gets installed, leading to a forwarding loop. Workaround: Enable rfc1583_compatability under the router ospf context.
cleaned_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what issue was resolved in OSPFv2 Bug 413286?

Workaround: Enable rfc1583_compatability under the router ospf context.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what issue was resolved in EVPN Bug 415063?
original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 415063): Symptom: After a VSX failover (following an MCLAG L2 port unshut event), EVPN MAC+IP Type-2 (RT2) routes for SVIs in the default VRF were not re-advertised to remote VTEP peers. This causes remote peers to lose reachability to hosts on those SVIs. Scenario: This issue occurs when the ARP/neighbor scan in ms_idl_bd_send_all_mac_ip_add_update() and ms_idl_bd_send_all_mac_ip_delete() is gated on port_row->vrf != NULL. For SVIs in the default VRF, the VRF column of the port row in OVSDB is NULL. This causes the entire neighbor scan loop to be silently skipped, preventing RT2 re-advertisement. Workaround: The VRF null check has been moved from the outer port-row check into the per-neighbor loop. Each neighbor entry is now individually guarded by !vrf_status_ready(nbr_row->vrf), which correctly handles default-VRF SVIs since nbr_row->vrf is non-NULL even for default-VRF neighbors. MAC+IP RT2 routes are now properly re-advertised after SVI recovery following a VSX failover event.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what issue was resolved in Central Connectivity Bug 412351?
original_answer: Central Connectivity (Bug ID 412351): Symptom: hpe-restd crashes when attempting to obtain Central location from Activate. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what known issue is documented for PBR Bug 407418?
original_answer: PBR (Bug ID 407418): Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 372373?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 372373): Symptom: Ethernet-tag mappings are permitted only when the operational state of the EVPN VLAN-aware bundle is disabled. Scenario: In previous versions of AOS-CX, modifications to VLAN: Ethernet-tag mappings were permitted only when the operational state of the EVPN VLAN-aware bundle was disabled. In AOS-CX 10.17, this restriction has been relaxed-modifications are now allowed regardless of the operational state, provided that the resulting effective Ethernet tag does not conflict with any existing VLAN tag assignment. This ensures configuration flexibility while maintaining tag uniqueness and preventing forwarding conflicts.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in Management Module Bug 384894?
original_answer: Management Module (Bug ID 384894): Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the swith, switch #1's port 1/1/2 will be assigned the identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two 6200, 6300, 6300L, 6400, 8100, or 8360 switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or don't use both of the ports with the overlap. Neither of those options is a feasible solution for a customer. Prior to the fix provided here, there is no explicit workaround for this issue.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in MSTP Bug 348612?
original_answer: MSTP (Bug ID 348612): Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in device-mode instead of client-mode: interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in Routing Bug 375775?
original_answer: Routing (Bug ID 375775): Symptom: Traffic loss in an SVI with vsx active-forwarding enabled, when vsx active-forwarding is disabled/removed on a entirely different SVI. Scenario: In a configuration where vsx active-forwarding is enabled, a special HW resource must be programmed for the VSX peers to handle traffic destined to the active-gateway IP. The trigger for this programming is tied to our router MAC programming (packets destined to this MAC that ingress on an L3 interface will perform an L3 lookup), and the ref counting for these router MACs had a bug. Since the ref counting would decrement early, we would remove our special HW resource and traffic loss can occur depending on which peer it lands on. Workaround: Unconfigure and reconfigure vsx active-forwarding in the SVI, or save the config and reboot the VSX peers.
cleaned_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in the Routing feature for Bug ID 375775?
cleaned_answer: Routing (Bug ID 375775): Symptom: Traffic loss in an SVI with vsx active-forwarding enabled when vsx active-forwarding is disabled/removed on a different SVI. Scenario: In a configuration where vsx active-forwarding is enabled, a special HW resource must be programmed for the VSX peers to handle traffic destined to the active-gateway IP. The trigger for this programming is tied to our router MAC programming (packets destined to this MAC that ingress on an L3 interface will perform an L3 lookup), and the ref counting for these router MACs had a bug. Since the ref counting would decrement early, we would remove our special HW resource and traffic loss can occur depending on which peer it lands on. Workaround: Unconfigure and reconfigure vsx active-forwarding in the SVI, or save the config and reboot the VSX peers.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what known issue is documented for PBR Bug 397544?
original_answer: PBR (Bug ID 397544): Symptom: A drop occurs when packet(s) match an applied policy with PBR default-nexthop action and the LPM/longest prefix matching route in the VRF for that packet is a route type that copies to CPU (unresolved, nullroute, reject or interface). Scenario: This issue can occur in the following workflows: A policy with PBR action is applied to a Layer 3 interface in a given VRF in the routed-in direction and the selected/active entry from the PBR action list is of type default-nexthop. A packet ingresses the system on the same Layer 3 interface and the packet is matched by the qualifiers in the policy's IPv4 or IPv6 class. There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what known issue is documented for Slot Management Bug 384894?
original_answer: Slot Management (Bug ID 384894): Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what issue was resolved in Central connectivity Bug 392076?
original_answer: Central connectivity (Bug ID 392076): Symptom: Switch is unable to connect to Central. Scenario: This issue may occur when attempting to onboard switches that are connected to Central. The process connecting to YANG is creating more sockets than expected, leading to overload of the yang-resolverd server and subsequent disconnections. Workaround: Restart yang-resolverd daemon to reestablish the operation of the daemon. Restart hpe-restd daemon to make a clean connection to YANG after restarting the yang-resolverd daemon.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what issue was resolved in VxLAN Bug 388259?
original_answer: VxLAN (Bug ID 388259): Symptom: Users experience a complete traffic drop for multicast and broadcast flows when the custom prefix is changed to /24, and the default range is also set to /24. Scenario: This issue occurs in a network setup involving multiple switches. When the custom prefix length and default length are both set to /24, multicast traffic fails to encapsulate properly, leading to a total loss of multicast traffic. Workaround: Clear the multicast route using clear ip mroute.

original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 408309): Symptom: SNMPv3 user password changes do not take effect, after deleting and re-creating the user with a new password, the device still accepts the old password and rejects the new one. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what known issue is documented for IPFIX Bug 408818?
original_answer: IPFIX (Bug ID 408818): Symptom: The MOD feature inspects packets punted to the local LC that are marked for drop. If IPFIX is enabled, these packets undergo further processing to match drop reasons (for example, IP exceptions or hardware drops). If IPFIX is not enabled, the system skips further processing and discards the memory allocated during inspection. Scenario: This issue occurs in a Multi-LC deployment, where there is a corner case where ingress and egress packets traverse different paths. When such a packet is discarded, a small memory leak occurs (approximately the size of hdf packet struct). Over time, these small per-packet leaks accumulate, gradually consuming more memory and potentially impacting the system.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what known issue is documented for MACSec Bug 402773?
original_answer: MACSec (Bug ID 402773): Symptom: When macsec selftest is executed on 25G MACsec uplink ports, macsec selftest is stuck in "Initialized" instead of "Passed". Scenario: This issue can occur when macsec selftest is executed on 25G MACsec uplink using the following steps: 1. On demand macsec selftest execution 2. Empty XCVR bay / 10G XCVR inserted in 25G MACsec uplink ports. Workaround: For on demand MACsec selftest to pass, do not use an empty port bay or 10G XCVRs. Alternatively, when an "Initialized" state is encountered, perform no macsec selftest to recover the port and reperform macsec selftest. Trigger MACsec selftest on boot: configuring macsec selftest and writing to memory followed by a reboot.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what issue was resolved in MAC Tables Bug 415095?
original_answer: MAC Tables (Bug ID 415095): Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur after resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

Scenario: This issue can occur after resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move between the two VSX devices, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what issue was resolved in OSPFv2 Bug 413286?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 413286): Symptom: OSPF default route in area 0 points to a stub router instead of ASBR via backbone area. Scenario: This issue can occur when there are two paths to the ASBR that generated the external route - one via stub area and another via non-stub area. The external route should get installed with the path reachable through the non-stub area, but the path via stub area gets installed, leading to a forwarding loop. Workaround: Enable rfc1583_compatability under the router ospf context.
cleaned_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what issue was resolved in OSPFv2 Bug 413286?

The external route should get installed with the path reachable through the non-stub area, but the path via stub area gets installed, leading to a forwarding loop. Workaround: Enable rfc1583_compatability under the router ospf context.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what issue was resolved in EVPN Bug 415063?
original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 415063): Symptom: After a VSX failover (following an MCLAG L2 port unshut event), EVPN MAC+IP Type-2 (RT2) routes for SVIs in the default VRF were not re-advertised to remote VTEP peers. This causes remote peers to lose reachability to hosts on those SVIs. Scenario: This issue occurs when the ARP/neighbor scan in ms_idl_bd_send_all_mac_ip_add_update() and ms_idl_bd_send_all_mac_ip_delete() is gated on port_row->vrf != NULL. For SVIs in the default VRF, the VRF column of the port row in OVSDB is NULL. This causes the entire neighbor scan loop to be silently skipped, preventing RT2 re-advertisement. Workaround: The VRF null check has been moved from the outer port-row check into the per-neighbor loop. Each neighbor entry is now individually guarded by !vrf_status_ready(nbr_row->vrf), which correctly handles default-VRF SVIs since nbr_row->vrf is non-NULL even for default-VRF neighbors. MAC+IP RT2 routes are now properly re-advertised after SVI recovery following a VSX failover event.
cleaned_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX release 10.17.1010, what issue was resolved in the EVPN feature related to Bug ID 415063?
cleaned_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 415063): Symptom: After a VSX failover following an MCLAG L2 port unshut event, EVPN MAC+IP Type-2 (RT2) routes for SVIs in the default VRF were not re-advertised to remote VTEP peers. This causes remote peers to lose reachability to hosts on those SVIs. Scenario: This issue occurs when the ARP/neighbor scan in ms_idl_bd_send_all_mac_ip_add_update() and ms_idl_bd_send_all_mac_ip_delete() is gated on port_row->vrf != NULL. For SVIs in the default VRF, the VRF column of the port row in OVSDB is NULL. This causes the entire neighbor scan loop to be silently skipped, preventing RT2 re-advertisement. Workaround: The VRF null check has been moved from the outer port-row check into the per-neighbor loop. Each neighbor entry is now individually guarded by !vrf_status_ready(nbr_row->vrf), which correctly handles default-VRF SVIs since nbr_row->vrf is non-NULL even for default-VRF neighbors. MAC+IP RT2 routes are now properly re-advertised after SVI recovery following a VSX failover event.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what is the workaround for EVPN Bug 415063?
original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 415063): The documented workaround is: The VRF null check has been moved from the outer port-row check into the per-neighbor loop. Each neighbor entry is now individually guarded by !vrf_status_ready(nbr_row->vrf), which correctly handles default-VRF SVIs since nbr_row->vrf is non-NULL even for default-VRF neighbors. MAC+IP RT2 routes are now properly re-advertised after SVI recovery following a VSX failover event.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what known issue is documented for PBR Bug 407418?
original_answer: PBR (Bug ID 407418): Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in Internal Services Bug 407591?
original_answer: Internal Services (Bug ID 407591): Symptom: Dual-port AP uplinks connected via a LAG intermittently show member interfaces as “Blocked by Port Access Security.” the LAG stops forwarding and the end device cannot onboard until the interfaces are toggled. Scenario: This issue may occur when port-access device-profile is enabled on the LAG with mode block-until-profile-applied, using LLDP for profiling. When the LAG member that had the LLDP neighbor goes Layer-1 down, subsequent authentication fails and the interface becomes blocked.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in Classifier Bug 366902?
original_answer: Classifier (Bug ID 366902): Symptom: Policy hitcounts commands using the vrf specifier do not include policies applied to L3 VNIs associated with the specified VRF. Scenario: This issue may occur when configuring an L3 VNI with a VRF, applying a policy to it, and sending traffic to increment hitcounts. Attempting to show policy hitcounts for the VRF fails and a report that no policy is applied to the VRF. Attempting to clear hitcounts for the VRF has no effect. Workaround: When showing or clearing hitcounts for all applications associated with the VRF, use a separate command to show or clear hitcounts for each L3 VNI.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 423594?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 423594): Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in PKI Bug 428981?
original_answer: PKI (Bug ID 428981): Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in UDP Forwarder Bug 418074?
original_answer: UDP Forwarder (Bug ID 418074): Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 410572?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 410572): Symptom: BGP peer group member sessions do not reset when the include-TCP-options setting is modified, while individual peer sessions reset as expected. This creates inconsistent behavior between peer group and standalone peer configurations. Scenario: This issue occurs when modifying or removing the include-TCP-options setting on a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured. Individual peer sessions respond correctly to the configuration change by resetting, but peer group member sessions remain established without resetting.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 404735?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 404735): Symptom: BGP session with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) remains in Established state even when a keychain key mismatch exists between peers. The session does not reset when the matching key is deleted from one peer, and manual session reset using clear bgp \* does not resolve the issue. This creates a security vulnerability as the session continues operating with mismatched authentication parameters. Scenario: This issue occurs when a matching keychain key is deleted from one BGP peer after both peers have been configured with the same key. The deletion creates a parameter mismatch between the two peers (different send-id, recv-id, or key string values), but the BGP session fails to detect this mismatch and remains established instead of resetting.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 359034?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 359034): Symptom: Attempts to unconfigure a keychain that is currently being used by a routing protocol (BGP with TCP-AO), result in the system displaying a confirmation message warning that the action disrupts protocol adjacencies. However, after confirming the removal by entering yes, the keychain is not actually deleted and remains in the configuration. Scenario: This issue occurs when trying to remove a keychain that is actively associated with a BGP neighbor configured with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO). The system presents a warning dialog and requests user confirmation, but fails to execute the deletion command even after confirmation is provided.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 414430?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 414430): Symptom: When BGP graceful restart is triggered on a TCP-AO authenticated BGP peer, the peer router does not mark the received routes as stale (indicated by the S prefix). Instead, routes are immediately removed from the routing table, causing a temporary loss of traffic visibility and convergence delay until the restarting peer comes back online. Scenario: This issue occurs when performing a graceful restart (redundancy switchover or hpe-routing restart) on a BGP peer that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with keychain authentication. The peer router fails to enter graceful restart helper mode and does not preserve routes with stale markers during the restart window.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what symptom occurs in BGP Bug 414430?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 414430): The symptom is: When BGP graceful restart is triggered on a TCP-AO authenticated BGP peer, the peer router does not mark the received routes as stale (indicated by the S prefix). Instead, routes are immediately removed from the routing table, causing a temporary loss of traffic visibility and convergence delay until the restarting peer comes back online.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, under what scenario does BGP Bug 414430 occur?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 414430): This issue occurs when performing a graceful restart (redundancy switchover or hpe-routing restart) on a BGP peer that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with keychain authentication. The peer router fails to enter graceful restart helper mode and does not preserve routes with stale markers during the restart window.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 410567?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 410567): Symptom: BGP peer group sessions with TCP-AO configured remain in Established state even after the include-TCP-options parameter is removed on one side. The sessions do not reset as expected, and they remain up even after manual session reset using clear bgp \*. Scenario: This issue occurs when the include-TCP-options setting is removed from a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with a keychain. While one side removes the parameter, the peer group member sessions on both sides continue to remain established instead of tearing down due to the configuration mismatch.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what known issue is documented for VSX Bug 425034?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 425034): Symptom: ISL connectivity may be affected as the full MTU override has not been applied to some ports. VSX connectivity between peers may be affected. Scenario: This issue may occur during bring up of ISL ports and may result in MTU override not being set correctly during port configuration. Ports that were missed during this configuration remain missed, even if more ports are added to ISL later. Workaround: Fully unconfigure the ISL and affected ports then reconfigure for proper MTU override to be applied.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what known issue is documented for DHCP Server Bug 420285?
original_answer: DHCP Server (Bug ID 420285): Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what known issue is documented for BGP Bug 341596?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 341596): Symptom: Routes take up to two minutes to switch to an alternate path when an active BGP neighbor is disabled, leading to delays in traffic rerouting. Scenario: This issue occurs in a network utilizing BGP routing. When a BGP session is disabled on one switch, the corresponding routes remain in a stale state until the stale path timer expires, causing traffic loss during the transition.

original_question: For 8100 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what known issue is documented for TPM Bug 426172?
original_answer: TPM (Bug ID 426172): Symptom: On switches running AOS-CX 10.18, a switch that is power-cycled or rebooted within 5 minutes of the login prompt after a zeroize operation, or within 5 minutes of the login prompt after updating to an affected 10.18 build, can encounter a TPM issue on the next boot. The switch may log TPM self-test or measurement errors and may reboot automatically several times before booting with Critical event_ID: 13603 'TPM selftest errors occurred on the current boot'. Scenario: This issue may occur on switches running AOS-CX 10.18 that are power-cycled or rebooted within 5 minutes of the login prompt after a zeroize operation, or within 5 minutes of the login prompt after updating to an affected 10.18 build. Workaround: Reboot or power cycle the switch and wait more than 5 minutes to power cycle or reboot.
